steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Defoe makes two paired claims about what historians must do. When a story has "sufficient authority," the historian must give the world "the special testimonial of its proper voucher" (credit the source). When it lacks authority, the historian must "say so." Both duties share the same principle: the historian must be transparent about how credible each source is.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Because no memories can be trusted, all historians are forced to admit that some records of events may be, in part, false.’: Defoe says unverified stories must be labeled as such, not that they are false. He does not claim all historians must confess to falsehoods. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The only figures that a historian should quote are those who are widely viewed as credible.’: Defoe never says historians must exclude weak sources — only that they must flag them. This is too restrictive. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘It is difficult for historians to be completely accurate, and eventually they will feel remorse for some of their work.’: No mention of remorse anywhere in the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Historians should clearly indicate the extent to which each of their sources is trustworthy.’: This captures Defoe's dual requirement — credit strong sources, flag weak ones — in a single principle: transparency about source credibility. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
